title: The Process of Radiography and the Electromagnetic Spectrum
narrationScript: Welcome to the start of the radiography technique course. Before we touch exposure charts or dose calculations, you need a clear picture of what a radiograph actually is and where the radiation that makes it comes from. Here is the whole process in one breath. A source on one side, a sheet of film on the other, and the part in between. The source sends out penetrating radiation that passes through the part. Thick or dense regions absorb more of the beam. Thin regions, voids, and low density inclusions absorb less. So the radiation arriving at the film is not uniform. It carries a map of everything it passed through. The film records that map as an invisible latent image, chemical processing turns the latent image into visible blackening, and a trained reader interprets the result on a high intensity illuminator. More radiation means more blackening. That is why a gas pore or a slag line shows up dark, and why weld reinforcement shows up light. Every technique decision you will ever make exists to sharpen that map and keep it honest. Where does the radiation come from. Two main families. X-ray machines make radiation electrically. Electrons are boiled off a hot filament, accelerated across a vacuum tube by high voltage, and slammed into a metal target. The sudden stop converts a small fraction of their energy into X-rays and roughly ninety nine percent into heat, which is why tubes have duty cycles and cooling. Turn off the voltage and the radiation stops completely. Gamma sources are radioactive isotopes such as iridium one ninety two and cobalt sixty. They emit gamma rays continuously as their atoms decay. No power needed, no off switch, and the output fades over time according to the half life. For very thick sections there are also high energy machines, linear accelerators and betatrons, that push X-ray energies into the million volt range. X-rays and gamma rays sit at the far end of the electromagnetic spectrum, the same family as radio waves, microwaves, and visible light. Everything in that family travels at the speed of light. What separates the bands is wavelength, and with it, energy. Radio waves are meters long. Visible light is around half a millionth of a meter. The radiation we use for radiography has wavelengths around a billionth of a meter and shorter, small enough to slip through the atomic spacing of solid metal. The rule to carry with you is simple. Shorter wavelength means higher energy, and higher energy means more penetrating radiation. Radiographers call high energy radiation hard and low energy radiation soft, and the word quality means the energy makeup of the beam, its penetrating character. Here is a point that trips up new technicians. An X-ray photon and a gamma photon of the same energy are physically identical. The only difference is the birthplace. X-rays are born in electron interactions outside the nucleus. Gamma rays are born inside the nucleus of a decaying atom. Once they leave, no instrument can tell them apart. The two families do differ in spectrum. An X-ray tube produces a continuous spread of energies, from nearly zero up to a maximum set by the tube voltage. Run the tube at two hundred kilovolts and the most energetic photon possible carries two hundred kiloelectron volts, with the bulk of the beam well below that. On top of the smooth curve sit a few sharp characteristic spikes tied to the target metal. A gamma isotope does the opposite. It emits only a few exact energies, like pure notes instead of a slide whistle. Iridium one ninety two emits a cluster of lines that average about a third of a million electron volts. Cobalt sixty emits two hard lines at about one point two and one point three million electron volts. And that means you cannot tune a gamma source. The isotope decides the quality. If you need different penetration, you pick a different isotope. With an X-ray machine you have two control knobs, and you must know exactly what each one does. The milliamperage knob sets the filament current, which sets how many electrons cross the tube, which sets how much radiation comes out. Double the milliamperage and you double the intensity, but the spectrum keeps its shape. Quality does not change. The kilovoltage knob sets how hard each electron hits the target. Raise it and the whole spectrum shifts toward higher energy and grows taller at the same time. The beam becomes more penetrating, and because more of it sails through every thickness, contrast between thicknesses drops while latitude grows. So remember it this way. Milliamperage controls quantity only. Kilovoltage controls quality and quantity together. Master those two sentences and every exposure chart you ever read will make sense.
imagePrompt: A radiographer in a fabrication shop positions a portable X-ray tube head on a tripod stand over a steel weld plate with a film cassette clamped beneath the weld, while the exposure control console with kilovoltage and milliamperage dials glows in the foreground.

sections.heading: The Radiographic Process, End to End
sections.body:
Film radiography is a shadow-recording process built on **differential absorption**. A source of penetrating radiation sits on one side of the part; a sheet of film in a light-tight cassette sits on the other. As the beam passes through the part, thick or dense regions absorb more of it, while thin regions, voids, and low-density inclusions absorb less. The radiation emerging from the back of the part is therefore no longer uniform - it carries an intensity map of everything it passed through.

The film records that map as a **latent image** - an invisible chemical change in the exposed silver halide grains. Processing (develop, stop, fix, wash, dry) converts the latent image into visible metallic-silver blackening, and the finished radiograph is read on a high-intensity illuminator. The governing rule: **more radiation reaching the film means more blackening**. A gas pore or slag inclusion transmits extra radiation and prints dark; weld reinforcement or a dense inclusion absorbs extra and prints light.

The complete process, start to finish:
- Select source, geometry, and exposure from the procedure and exposure chart
- Set up source, part, and film with IQIs and identification markers
- Expose, then process the film under controlled conditions
- View and interpret the radiograph, then disposition against the acceptance standard

sections.caption: Differential absorption maps the part onto the film: thick sections print light, thin sections and voids print dark.

sections.heading: Two Families of Radiation Sources
sections.body:
**X-ray machines** generate radiation electrically. A heated filament boils off electrons; high voltage accelerates them across an evacuated tube into a metal target, where the sudden deceleration converts a small fraction of their energy into X-rays - and roughly **99% into heat**, which is why tubes have duty cycles, cooling systems, and warm-up procedures. The output is fully adjustable through kVp and mA, and it stops completely when the voltage is switched off.

**Gamma-ray sources** are radioactive isotopes. The workhorses are **Ir-192** (half-life about 74 days, average energy about 0.35 MeV, suited to steel roughly 0.5 to 2.5 in.) and **Co-60** (5.27 years, 1.17 and 1.33 MeV, steel roughly 1.5 to 8 in.), with **Se-75** (about 120 days, lower energy) favored for thin-wall, high-contrast work. They need no power and no cooling, which makes them supremely portable - but they emit continuously. There is no off switch, only the shielded position inside the exposure device, and output decays with time.

For very thick sections, **high-energy machines** - linear accelerators and betatrons - push X-ray energies into the MeV range and penetrate steel far beyond the reach of conventional tubes.

sections.heading: The Electromagnetic Spectrum and Beam Quality
sections.body:
X-rays and gamma rays belong to the **electromagnetic spectrum** - the same wave family as radio, microwave, infrared, visible light, and ultraviolet. All of it travels at the speed of light; what distinguishes the bands is **wavelength**, and with it frequency and photon energy. Energy rises as wavelength shrinks (E = hf, with f = c/λ).

Radio waves span meters; visible light sits near 4-7 × 10⁻⁷ m; the radiation used for industrial radiography lies around **10⁻⁹ to 10⁻¹³ m** - short enough to pass through solid metal that stops light cold.

In radiography, **quality** means the energy makeup of the beam - its penetrating character:
- **Higher energy (shorter wavelength) = harder, more penetrating** radiation, lower subject contrast, wider latitude
- **Lower energy (longer wavelength) = softer** radiation, less penetration, higher subject contrast

Penetrating ability is the practical consequence of position on the spectrum: a 1 MeV photon punches through steel that stops a 100 keV photon almost completely. Choosing beam quality - by setting kVp on a tube or selecting an isotope - is the radiographer's first technique decision, because it fixes both what you can penetrate and how much contrast the image will carry.

sections.caption: Industrial radiography uses the shortest-wavelength, highest-energy end of the electromagnetic spectrum.

sections.title: Same photon, different birthplace
sections.body: An X-ray photon and a gamma-ray photon of equal energy are **physically identical** - same speed, same penetrating behavior, same effect on film. The only difference is origin: **X-rays** arise from electron interactions outside the nucleus (deceleration at a target, orbital transitions); **gamma rays** are emitted from the **nucleus** of a decaying radioactive atom. Once emitted, no instrument can tell them apart - energy, not birthplace, governs behavior.

sections.heading: Two Spectra: Continuous vs. Line
sections.body:
An X-ray tube emits a **continuous (bremsstrahlung) spectrum**. Electrons arrive at the target with energy set by the tube voltage, and each gives up some or all of it as it decelerates - so photon energies spread from near zero up to a hard ceiling equal to the peak kilovoltage: at **220 kVp the maximum photon energy is 220 keV**. Most of the beam carries far less; the **effective energy** of a continuous spectrum is roughly **one-third to one-half of the peak**. Superimposed on the smooth curve are **characteristic lines** - sharp spikes at energies fixed by the target metal. For tungsten, the K-series near 59-69 keV appears once the tube exceeds about 70 kVp.

A **gamma source emits a line spectrum**: a few discrete energies determined entirely by the isotope's nuclear structure. Ir-192 emits a cluster of lines spanning roughly 0.2 to 0.6 MeV with an average near **0.35 MeV**; Co-60 emits two hard lines at **1.17 and 1.33 MeV**; Se-75 averages near 0.21 MeV.

The practical consequence: **gamma quality cannot be adjusted**. No knob changes a nuclear energy level - if the job needs different penetration or contrast, you change isotopes or switch to an X-ray machine.

sections.caption: An X-ray tube produces a continuous spectrum capped at the kVp with characteristic spikes; a gamma isotope emits only discrete lines.

sections.heading: The Two Knobs: mA and kVp
sections.body:
An X-ray operator controls the beam with two settings, and they do different jobs.

**Milliamperage (mA)** sets the filament current, which sets how many electrons cross the tube per second, which sets **intensity (quantity)** - and nothing else. Double the mA and the spectrum keeps exactly the same shape and maximum energy; every point simply doubles in height. Because intensity is directly proportional to mA, exposures are reckoned in **mA·min**: 5 mA for 4 minutes delivers the same exposure as 10 mA for 2 minutes.

**Kilovoltage (kVp)** sets how hard each electron strikes the target, and it changes **quality and quantity together**. Raising kVp pushes the maximum and effective energies upward - a harder, more penetrating beam - and total intensity climbs at the same time, faster than the voltage itself. The radiographic trade: higher kVp lowers subject contrast and widens latitude; lower kVp raises contrast but limits penetration.

Working rules:
- Choose **kVp for the material and thickness** - penetration and contrast
- Choose **mA and time for the density** - quantity of exposure
- kVp is the coarse knob: a small voltage change moves film density dramatically, so density trims are normally made with mA·min

figures.title: X-ray continuous spectrum vs. gamma line spectrum

interactive.title: mA or kVp - Which Knob Does That?
interactive.intro: Your tube is running at 60 kVp and 5 mA. For each effect below, decide which control change produces it: raising mA with kVp fixed, or raising kVp with mA fixed.

items.label: Beam intensity increases but the spectrum keeps the same shape and energy ceiling
items.why: mA sets electron current, so it scales photon quantity without touching photon energies - quality is unchanged.

items.label: The highest-energy photons in the beam become more energetic
items.why: The spectrum's energy ceiling equals the peak kilovoltage, so only kVp can move it.

items.label: The beam becomes harder and penetrates thicker steel
items.why: Penetrating quality is set by photon energy, which only kVp controls.

items.label: Exposure time can be cut proportionally with no change in contrast
items.why: Reciprocity: mA × time gives the same exposure, and since mA leaves quality alone, contrast holds.

items.label: Subject contrast drops and latitude widens
items.why: A harder spectrum flattens the absorption difference between thicknesses - the classic high-kVp trade.

items.label: Tungsten characteristic lines appear where there were none
items.why: Characteristic K-lines appear only when electron energy exceeds the shell binding energy - about 70 kVp for tungsten.

items.label: Electron current from filament to target increases
items.why: The mA reading literally is the measure of electron current crossing the tube.

items.label: Intensity rises and the effective energy shifts upward at the same time
items.why: kVp is the only control that changes quantity and quality together - output grows faster than the voltage.

checkQuestions.question: Why does a gas pore in a weld appear as a dark spot on the radiograph?
options: The pore absorbs more radiation, so less reaches the film
The pore is a void, so more radiation passes through and produces more film blackening
Gas fluoresces under the beam and exposes the film directly
The pore scatters all radiation away from the film
checkQuestions.explanation: Radiography works by differential absorption: a void removes absorber from the beam path, so more radiation reaches the film there, and more exposure means more blackening.

checkQuestions.question: An X-ray photon and a gamma-ray photon each carry 0.5 MeV. What distinguishes them?
options: The gamma ray is more penetrating
Only their origin - electron interactions outside the nucleus versus decay of the nucleus
Their wavelengths differ
The X-ray travels faster
checkQuestions.explanation: Photons of equal energy are physically identical regardless of source; X-rays and gamma rays differ only in where they are generated.

checkQuestions.question: In radiographic work, the 'quality' of a beam refers to:
options: The number of photons emitted per second
The physical size of the focal spot
Its energy distribution and penetrating character
The film density it produces
checkQuestions.explanation: Quality describes the energy makeup of the beam - how hard or soft, how penetrating - while intensity describes the quantity of radiation.

checkQuestions.question: A tube is operated at 200 kVp. The maximum photon energy in the beam is:
options: 67 keV
100 keV
200 keV
400 keV
checkQuestions.explanation: The continuous spectrum ends at an energy equal to the peak tube voltage - 200 kVp gives a 200 keV ceiling - while the effective energy is only about one-third to one-half of that.

checkQuestions.question: The operator doubles the mA while leaving kVp unchanged. What happens to the beam?
options: Penetrating ability doubles
Intensity doubles; quality and maximum energy are unchanged
Both maximum energy and intensity double
Nothing changes until kVp is also raised
checkQuestions.explanation: mA controls only the electron current, so it scales intensity in direct proportion while the spectrum shape - and therefore quality and contrast - stays the same.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiography Technique 1.1-1.7
General radiographic physics: electromagnetic spectrum, bremsstrahlung and characteristic X-ray production, gamma decay spectra
Typical industrial exposure-chart practice for X-ray tubes (kV vs. thickness at fixed mA·min)
